result.markdown:
---
title: "Statistical Analysis"
subtitle: "Methodology · Part 2"
author: "Team HeeHaa"
date: last-modified
---

The analysis turns the prepared tables into three behavioural signals. Each signal is computed against a baseline period the analyst chooses, so that what counts as normal is explicit and adjustable rather than fixed in advance.

## Defining the baseline

The analyst sets a single round as the end of the baseline period. Rounds up to that point form the calm period and later rounds form the suspect period. The default boundary sits at round 20, which places the calm period across the pre-crisis stretch and leaves the late crisis-day rounds under suspicion. Moving the boundary recomputes every signal, so the whole investigation can be re-run under a different definition of normal.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-summary="Splitting the timeline into calm and suspect periods"}
msgs <- messages_tbl |>
  mutate(period = factor(
    if_else(round_idx <= split, "Calm period", "Suspect period"),
    levels = c("Calm period", "Suspect period")))
```
:::


## Abnormality scoring

The abnormality signal measures how far each agent departs from its own habitual channel use. For the calm period, the share of an agent's messages that fell on each channel was computed. This share is that agent's normal pattern. A channel an agent used heavily in the calm period has a high share, and a channel it never touched has a share of zero.

The unusualness of a channel for an agent is one minus that share. A message is flagged when the share for its agent and channel falls below a strictness threshold the analyst controls, with a default of five percent. A lower threshold flags only the most out-of-character messages and a higher one flags more.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-summary="Per-agent calm-period channel shares, then flagging unusual messages"}
agent_channel_baseline <- function(df, last_base) {
  base <- df |> filter(round_idx <= last_base)
  total_by_agent <- base |> count(agent_id, name = "agent_total")
  base |>
    count(agent_id, channel, name = "n") |>
    left_join(total_by_agent, by = "agent_id") |>
    mutate(share = n / agent_total)   # share of this agent's calm-period traffic
}

score <- function(df, last_base, strict) {
  sh <- agent_channel_baseline(df, last_base) |> select(agent_id, channel, share)
  df |>
    left_join(sh, by = c("agent_id", "channel")) |>
    mutate(share = replace_na(share, 0), flagged = share < strict / 100)
}
```
:::


A robustness check guards against the finding being an artefact of where the boundary was drawn. The count of flagged messages in the suspect period under the chosen baseline is compared to the count under an earlier baseline. When the two counts are close the finding does not depend much on the boundary, and when they diverge the finding is sensitive to it and is reported as such.

## Bypass detection

The bypass signal is the central one. It looks for moments an agent reached the outside world through a channel the Judge cannot see. Every post on an unmonitored public channel is examined and labelled by whether anything accountable could account for it.

For each such post, the method checks whether the same agent left a trace on a monitored channel in the post's own round or within a chosen number of preceding rounds. When no monitored trace falls in that window the post is a stronger bypass, because no accountable discussion the Judge could see sits near it. When a monitored trace does fall in the window the post is likely explained, because accountable discussion was visible nearby. The window defaults to two rounds and is adjustable.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-summary="Classifying each unmonitored public post against the monitored-trace window"}
detect_bypass <- function(df, window_n) {
  mon_rounds <- df |>
    filter(channel %in% monitored_channels) |>
    distinct(agent_id, round_idx) |> mutate(mon = TRUE)

  df |>
    filter(channel %in% public_unmonitored) |>
    rowwise() |>
    mutate(
      accounted = any(mon_rounds$agent_id == agent_id &
                      mon_rounds$round_idx >= (round_idx - window_n) &
                      mon_rounds$round_idx <= round_idx),
      strength  = if_else(!accounted, "Stronger bypass", "Likely explained")
    ) |>
    ungroup()
}
```
:::


## Network difference

The network signal treats communication as relationships rather than counts. Directed agent-to-agent edges were built from the recipient lists, with each edge weighted by how many messages passed along it. For any two rounds the analyst selects, the method compares the two edge sets and labels every link as new in the later round, dropped before it, or present in both. The same comparison is available in a bipartite form that links agents to the channels they used, which exposes drift toward the low-accountability channels as a change in structure.

A per-agent degree, the total number of links touching each agent in a round, sizes the nodes. A short summary names the agent that lost the most links between the two compared rounds, which surfaces an agent moving from the centre of the conversation to its edge.

## Topic tagging

To connect the signals back to subject matter, every message was tagged with each topic whose keyword pattern its content matches. Tagging is many-to-many, so a message touching both merger and compliance language carries both topics. The tags drive two complementary views, one moving from a round to the topics discussed in it and one moving from a topic to every round, agent and channel where it appeared. The second view is what surfaces an early warning, because it shows the first round a sensitive topic reached an unmonitored channel.

The output of this stage is a set of signals ready to be shown through the four interactive sections described under [Data Exploration & Visualisation](data-exploration.qmd).
